items.q: What does 'Rethink' mean in the 6 Rs?
items.correct: Questioning whether a product is necessary or could be designed differently for sustainability.
items.misconception: Trying a design again if it does not work first time.
items.why: Students apply 'rethink' to design iteration. In sustainability, it means challenging the NEED for a product altogether, or fundamentally rethinking how it's made - a more radical step than reducing materials.

items.q: What determines whether a timber is a hardwood or softwood?
items.correct: The type of tree - deciduous (hardwood) or coniferous (softwood), not its hardness.
items.misconception: Whether the wood itself is physically hard or soft.
items.random_wrong: How long the tree took to grow.
items.why: Students assume 'hardwood' means hard. Balsa is a hardwood but very soft; yew is a softwood but harder than many hardwoods. Classification is BOTANICAL, not based on hardness.

items.q: What property do shape memory alloys have?
items.correct: They return to a pre-set shape when heated.
items.misconception: They remember every shape they have ever been bent into.
items.why: Students think SMAs are infinitely programmable. They actually have ONE 'remembered' shape, set during high-temperature treatment. Heating restores that shape; the alloy doesn't store a history of deformations.
Correct answer: Shape Memory Alloy. “Smart Modern Alloy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Light. “Heat” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many smart material types are listed in this lesson?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
